finding_title: Undefined completeness criteria for Operational Authorisation pack
text_excerpt: As a CAA case officer I want to receive a complete Operational Authorisation pack so that I can open a valid case. (REQ001)
interpretation_a: The case officer will judge the pack complete when they perceive all necessary documentation is present (subjective threshold)
interpretation_b: There exists a definitive checklist of required documents, items, or evidence that determines completeness (objective threshold)
reasoning: The entire regulatory intake process hinges on this undefined threshold. If completeness is subjective, different case officers may accept different submissions. If objective, the checklist is not provided. Dorothy glossary confirms 'complete' is unverified with no criteria given. This is the foundation requirement—failure cascades.
recommendation: Define 'complete Operational Authorisation pack' with an explicit enumerated checklist of required documents, each with specified format, version, and submission rules. Reference a normative document (e.g., CAP 722 Section X) or create a dedicated submission template.

finding_title: Passive voice obscures accountability for 'defensibly' setting SAIL and OSO
text_excerpt: As a CAA SORA assessor I want swarm-aware SORA evidence so that I can set SAIL and OSO requirements defensibly. (REQ002)
interpretation_a: The assessor must demonstrate their decision-making process can withstand regulatory or legal scrutiny, but no criteria for such scrutiny is specified.
interpretation_b: Defensibility is measured against external standards (e.g., legal precedent, technical consensus), but the standard is left implicit.
reasoning: The word 'defensibly' is load-bearing in a safety-critical decision (setting SAIL/OSO). It hides the actor (who judges defensibility?) and the standard (defensible against whom? a court? peer review? regulator audit?). Dorothy glossary marks 'defensibly' as high-stakes and undefined. Without clarification, assessors cannot know what threshold they must meet and regulators cannot verify compliance.
recommendation: Replace 'defensibly' with explicit criterion: 'I can set SAIL and OSO requirements supported by [quantitative risk model / expert panel consensus / peer-reviewed analysis / regulatory precedent]. Example: 'I can set SAIL and OSO requirements that satisfy the JARUS SORA v2.5 risk matrix and are documented in the SORA Case File.'

finding_title: 'Swarm-aware SORA evidence' undefined—distinction from standard SORA unclear
text_excerpt: As a CAA SORA assessor I want swarm-aware SORA evidence so that I can set SAIL and OSO requirements defensibly. (REQ002)
interpretation_a: Additional SORA evidence specific to multi-aircraft collective behaviour beyond what standard single-aircraft SORA would require
interpretation_b: Standard SORA assessment applies, but the applicant must explicitly address swarm-specific risk scenarios (e.g., loss of inter-vehicle comms, emergent behaviour)
reasoning: SORA is designed for individual aircraft. Swarms introduce collective risks not captured in single-aircraft SORA: loss of inter-vehicle coordination, emergent behaviour, cascading failures. 'Swarm-aware' implies a delta to standard SORA, but the delta is undefined. Dorothy glossary marks 'swarm-aware SORA evidence' as critical and unverified. The SORA assessor cannot know what additional evidence to demand.
recommendation: Define 'swarm-aware SORA evidence' with explicit additions to JARUS SORA v2.5: e.g., 'Evidence of swarm de-confliction logic (Section 4.2), inter-vehicle link loss procedures (Section 4.3), and emergent behaviour validation (Appendix B).' Provide reference to a swarm-specific SORA extension or create a normative appendix.

finding_title: 'Clear ConOps and ODD'—clarity criteria undefined; audience unclear
text_excerpt: As a CAA safety-case reviewer I want a clear ConOps and ODD so that I can judge nominal and worst-credible behaviour. (REQ003)
interpretation_a: 'Clear' means the reviewer can understand the document without domain expertise
interpretation_b: 'Clear' means the document contains all information necessary for a domain expert to judge the stated behaviours (reviewer assumed expert)
reasoning: Clarity is subjective and audience-dependent. A ConOps clear to an aerospace engineer may be unclear to a procurement specialist. Dorothy glossary notes 'clear' is a vague quantifier with no audience specified. The reviewer's assessment of clarity cannot be verified or objectified.
recommendation: Replace 'clear' with specific quality criteria: 'A ConOps and ODD that conforms to [standard template], includes [sections X, Y, Z], defines all acronyms on first use, and provides a formal specification of nominal and worst-credible behaviour boundaries using [quantitative metrics / decision trees / state diagrams].'

finding_title: Distinction between 'nominal' and 'worst-credible' behaviour undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA safety-case reviewer I want a clear ConOps and ODD so that I can judge nominal and worst-credible behaviour. (REQ003)
interpretation_a: Nominal = expected performance under normal conditions; worst-credible = most severe foreseeable failure with quantifiable probability threshold (e.g., > 1e-6 / flight hour)
interpretation_b: Nominal = planned behaviour; worst-credible = any adverse condition that could plausibly occur, with no quantitative boundary
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'worst-credible' as unverified with ambiguous boundary: 'what is credible vs incredible? Who judges this? No quantitative threshold given.' Without a quantitative boundary (e.g., failure rate, frequency), the reviewer cannot determine which failure modes must be covered. This creates regulatory inconsistency.
recommendation: Define worst-credible behaviour with explicit criteria: 'Failure modes with failure rate >= [10^-6 | 10^-7 / flight hour] or those identified in DO-178C or similar aviation safety standard.' Alternatively, provide a formal credibility assessment method (e.g., expert panel, historical data).

finding_title: 'Automatic containment'—trigger and scope undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA airspace regulator I want automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS. (REQ005)
interpretation_a: Containment enforces hard limits on aircraft position; triggered automatically on loss of C2 or autonomy failure (but trigger list not exhaustive)
interpretation_b: Containment continuously enforces boundaries; triggered by any deviation from ODD, monitored by autonomous system with no operator involvement
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'containment' as unverified with no specification of tolerance, fail-safe assumptions, or design standards. 'Automatic' lacks trigger criteria: loss of C2? Autonomy failure? Geofence breach? Each interpretation implies different system architecture. Is containment a hard stop (reject commands outside bounds) or soft limit (warn then enforce)? No definition provided.
recommendation: Specify containment with explicit triggers and mechanisms: 'Automatic containment shall enforce airspace boundaries by [hard-coded geofence limits | monitored by independent FPGA | enforced by loss-of-signal failsafe]. Containment triggers on: (1) loss of C2 for > [X seconds], (2) autonomy detection of out-of-ODD condition, (3) [other conditions]. Containment tolerance band: [±Y meters].'

finding_title: 'Authorised monitored C2'—actor and compliance threshold undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA spectrum specialist I want authorised monitored C2 and inter-vehicle links so that link loss is survivable and non-interfering. (REQ006)
interpretation_a: 'Authorised' by spectrum authority (Ofcom); 'monitored' by ground crew; failure thresholds and non-compliance actions undefined
interpretation_b: 'Authorised' by trial approval process; 'monitored' autonomously by UAS; failure detection and response automatic
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks both 'authorised' and 'monitored' as unverified with no actor defined. Who authorises? Who monitors? What triggers intervention? What are failure thresholds (bit error rate? latency? signal strength)? If authority and monitoring mechanisms differ, the C2 assurance model is undefined. This is load-bearing for non-interference assurance.
recommendation: Specify C2 authority and monitoring: 'C2 shall be authorised by [Ofcom spectrum license]. Monitoring shall be performed by [ground crew using [telemetry display / autonomous link-quality monitor]]. Link quality thresholds: [BER < 10^-6, latency < 100ms, signal strength > -80 dBm]. Non-compliance trigger: [operator intervention / automatic mode-down / safe termination] within [X seconds].'

finding_title: 'Resolved predictably'—resolution criteria and confidence level undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA DAA assessor I want DO-365 / F3442-aligned evidence for each aircraft and coordinated swarm de-confliction so that cooperative and non-cooperative encounters are resolved predictably. (REQ007)
interpretation_a: Avoidance maneuver succeeds reliably (>99.9% of test scenarios) with consistent geometry and timing
interpretation_b: Avoidance outcome is deterministic given input conditions (but may include crew-selected options)
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'resolved predictably' as unverified and 'highly subjective.' What counts as resolution? Avoidance executed? Separation maintained? Crew takeover enabled? At what confidence level? DO-365 and F3442 are referenced but their requirements are not reproduced. The assessor cannot verify predictability without quantitative thresholds or acceptance criteria.
recommendation: Define resolution with quantitative metrics: 'Cooperative encounters shall be resolved predictably, achieving [MINIMUM X meters separation] in [≥99.9% | ≥99.99%] of test scenarios per DO-365 Section [X]. Resolution time: [≤ Y seconds from detection]. Non-cooperative encounters shall be resolved with [evasive maneuver | crew takeover] within [Z seconds], per F3442 [Section].'

finding_title: 'Airborne limits'—type and setter undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA autonomy reviewer I want the decision-making architecture airborne limits and emergent-behaviour evidence so that I can assure what the swarm will do collectively. (REQ008)
interpretation_a: Hard constraints on autonomous decisions (e.g., max altitude, speed); set by autonomy designer, enforced onboard
interpretation_b: Soft advisory limits on crew decisions; set by regulator during authorization; monitored by system but not enforced
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'airborne limits' as unverified and 'ambiguous: are these hard constraints, soft limits, or advisory bounds? Who sets them?' Hard vs. soft distinction is critical for autonomy assurance. Different limit types imply different failure modes and recovery procedures. The autonomy reviewer cannot assess assurance without knowing limit mechanism.
recommendation: Specify airborne limits with type and authority: 'The swarm autonomous decision-making shall be constrained by [hard limits | soft limits | advisory limits] on: [altitude, airspeed, turn rate, acceleration]. These limits shall be [set by autonomy designer and embedded in code | set by CAA during authorization and enforced by [monitor | enforcement module]]. Hard limits shall be [immutable during flight | modifiable only post-flight]. Soft limits shall trigger [alert | mode-down | crew intervention].'

finding_title: 'Forensically explainable' ML—forensic standard undefined; passive voice hides reviewer
text_excerpt: As a CAA AI/ML assurance specialist I want an AMLAS / SACE case a frozen-model manifest and explainability evidence so that ML components are assurable and forensically explainable. (REQ009)
interpretation_a: ML decisions can be traced to input data and model parameters post-incident using reconstruction tools (e.g., model weights, test inputs)
interpretation_b: ML decisions can be justified to accident investigators using domain expertise in deep learning and model interpretation techniques
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'forensically explainable' as unverified, critical, and high-stakes: 'Forensically' implies legal/investigative standard but scope and methods undefined. Different forensic standards (legal discovery, safety investigation, engineering audit) imply different evidence burdens. Passive voice ('are...explainable') hides the actor: who must explain? The ML engineer? A domain expert? An automated tool?
recommendation: Define forensic explainability with explicit method: 'ML components shall be forensically explainable, meaning that in the event of an incident, the CAA accident investigator can reconstruct the ML decision via: (1) Frozen model weights and architecture, (2) Input feature values from flight data, (3) [Saliency maps | attention weights | decision trees] showing feature influence on output. Explainability shall meet [NTSB AI guidance | ISO/IEC 42001 transparency requirements | [custom criteria]].'

finding_title: 'Meaningful human control'—borrowed from LAWS debate; no civil aviation definition exists
text_excerpt: As a CAA human factors inspector I want workload duty and take-over evidence so that meaningful human control is demonstrated throughout. (REQ010)
interpretation_a: Crew retain override capability for every autonomous decision and can exercise it before safety-critical outcomes occur (high bar)
interpretation_b: Crew maintain situational awareness and monitor autonomy, with takeover capability available but not used unless needed (moderate bar)
reasoning: Dorothy glossary notes 'meaningful human control' is 'heavily debated term. No aviation standard in civil aviation.' The phrase originates in international humanitarian law (LAWS treaty debate on autonomous weapons) and has no consensus definition in civil aviation. Different CAA inspectors could interpret this radically differently. The vague timing 'throughout' compounds the ambiguity: does this mean continuous direct control, periodic review, or exception-based intervention?
recommendation: Replace 'meaningful human control' with specific, measurable criteria: 'Human crew shall demonstrate control via: (1) [Situational awareness demonstrated by workload assessment per NASA TLX ≤ X | Crew decision-making time ≤ Y seconds], (2) Takeover capability with [latency ≤ Z seconds | success rate ≥ 99% in simulation]. Takeover shall be required for decisions involving: [phase change | airspace boundary | contingency response]. Crew monitoring shall occur [continuously | at intervals ≤ N seconds] per [CAA-approved workload model].'

finding_title: 'Independent termination'—independence scope undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA flight-ops inspector I want independent termination a contingency matrix and ATSU coordination so that the trial can be made safe in any off-nominal state. (REQ011)
interpretation_a: Termination function that operates independently of C2, crew, and onboard autonomy (three independent channels)
interpretation_b: Termination function independent of normal flight control (but may share power, avionics, or data with other systems)
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'independent termination' as unverified: 'Independence criteria and test methods undefined. Relationship to containment unclear.' Independence from what exactly? C2 link? Crew commands? Autonomy system? Power supply? All three? How independent (separate hardware? separate power? architectural isolation?)? The relationship to 'automatic containment' (REQ005) is unclear: are these redundant, complementary, or mutually exclusive?
recommendation: Define independent termination: 'Flight termination shall be independent of: (1) C2 link (no ground commands required), (2) Onboard autonomy system, (3) Main power system (fail-safe timer or battery-backed system). Independence shall be verified by [fault injection testing | safety analysis per DO-178C Level A]. Termination shall be triggered by [geofence breach, loss of C2 for > X seconds, autonomy watchdog timeout]. Relationship to containment (REQ005): [Containment prevents exit; Termination ensures safe landing upon exit].'

finding_title: 'Complete recorded evidence'—scope and retention period undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA flight safety investigator I want complete recorded evidence accepted MOR obligations and a DPIA so that any incident is investigable and privacy is protected. (REQ012)
interpretation_a: All data from all sensors at highest available fidelity (video, telemetry, ML logs) retained indefinitely
interpretation_b: Minimum data required by MOR regulations at sufficient fidelity for accident investigation, retained for regulatory period (e.g., 5 years)
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'complete' as unverified with no criteria in REQ012 context. 'Complete' could mean: all sensor streams? summary statistics? video only? Data rates vary by orders of magnitude (10 Hz telemetry vs. 60 Hz video vs. 1 kHz accelerometers). Retention period undefined: forensic investigation may need indefinite retention; GDPR privacy may limit retention to 2-3 years. Storage cost and legal risk scale with completeness and duration.
recommendation: Specify recorded evidence: 'Complete recorded evidence shall include: (1) Aircraft states [altitude, position, velocity] at [≥1 Hz], (2) Crew inputs and alerts at [real-time], (3) C2 link status [signal strength, BER] at [≥0.1 Hz], (4) Autonomy decision logs [timestamped, decision rationale] at [real-time], (5) Video [from payload] at [≥30 fps]. Retention: [MOR incidents: indefinite; routine flights: 12 months post-flight]. Storage: [on-board + cloud | redundant]. Privacy: data anonymization per DPIA shall remove [crew/observer identities | ground-person identities] from [48 hours post-flight | before CAA release].'

finding_title: 'Factually grounded' third-party risk—evidence standard undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA authorisation officer I want insurance and a ground survey so that third-party risk is both transferable and factually grounded. (REQ013)
interpretation_a: Risk assessment based on measured ground conditions (survey data, actual population density, observed infrastructure)
interpretation_b: Risk assessment grounded in probabilistic model calibrated to empirical data (historical accident rates, validated simulation)
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'factually grounded' as unverified: 'Qualitative criterion. Factually implies verification standard but method undefined.' Different reviewers may judge what counts as 'fact'—surveyed ground data, historical UAS accident statistics, simulation predictions. The standard is discipline-dependent (insurance may trust actuarial data; CAA may demand direct observation; engineers may use models).
recommendation: Define factually grounded with explicit evidence: 'Third-party risk shall be factually grounded via: (1) Documented ground survey [date, aerial imagery, population density map, infrastructure assessment per CAP 722 Section X], (2) Risk calculation [per SORA model | per [insurance standard]], (3) Validation [comparison to historical accident data | peer expert review]. Risk shall be documented as [failure rate in accidents/flight-hour], not as qualitative assessment.'

finding_title: 'Evidenced competency'—evidence type and domain undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA licensing inspector I want evidenced competency so that the trial is flown by qualified crew. (REQ014)
interpretation_a: Competency evidenced through existing UAS BVLOS licenses, supplemented by swarm-specific training and sign-off
interpretation_b: Competency evidenced through new qualification exam or simulator evaluation specific to autonomous swarms
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'evidenced competency' as unverified: 'Evidence types and standards for BVLOS swarm operations undefined. Existing licenses adequate or new qualification needed?' Swarm operations introduce novel decision-making (distributed control, inter-vehicle comms failure, emergent behaviour) not covered in standard BVLOS licensing. Which evidence counts? Logbook hours in manned aviation? Prior BVLOS experience? Simulator performance? No standard exists for swarm competency assessment.
recommendation: Specify competency evidence: 'Crew competency shall be evidenced via: (1) [Existing BVLOS license per CAP 722, category: [ACCA-Level 1 | Level 2]], (2) Swarm-specific qualification: [written exam ≥ X% pass rate | simulator evaluation ≥ Y scenarios at ≥ Z% success] per [training curriculum], (3) Endorsement signature from [type rating instructor | CAA examiner]. Competency domains: [basic airmanship | swarm dynamics | autonomy limits | contingency management]. Recurrent training: [annually | per incident].'

finding_title: 'Signed apportionment' and 'gap-free'—signatory and gap criteria undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA/MAA liaison I want a signed apportionment so that regulatory ownership is gap-free. (REQ015)
interpretation_a: Signed by CAA and MAA (Ministry of Aviation Authority or similar military authority); gap-free means every aspect assigned to one authority
interpretation_b: Signed by CAA and co-regulator (MOD/MAA); gap-free means no overlap or ambiguity in decision authority
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'apportionment' as unverified: 'Specific allocation between CAA and MAA undefined. Document form and approval process unknown.' 'Gap-free' is undefined: what constitutes a gap? Overlapping jurisdiction? Conflicting requirements? Unclear decision authority? The signatory authority is ambiguous (CAA yes, but MAA is not defined). The document is implied but format not specified.
recommendation: Specify apportionment: 'A signed Apportionment Document shall be agreed between [CAA and MOD/MAA], assigning regulatory authority over: (1) BVLOS authorization and ongoing compliance [CAA], (2) Classified MOD evidence handling and security assurance [MOD/MAA], (3) [Other domains]. The document shall be gap-free, meaning: [each function assigned to exactly one authority | overlapping authority resolved by [joint approval threshold]]. Apportionment shall be signed by [CAA Head of Delegation and MOD/MAA Head of Delegation]. Format: [form per CAA template | Apportionment Matrix]. Review trigger: [annually | upon trial scope change].'

finding_title: 'Phased gates' and 'reversible'—phase definitions and reversal triggers undefined
text_excerpt: As a CAA programme manager I want phased gates so that evidence builds progressively and is reversible. (REQ016)
interpretation_a: Fixed sequence of 3-5 phases (e.g., single-aircraft, 2-aircraft, swarm); reversal triggered only on incident or safety finding
interpretation_b: Flexible phases defined per trial profile; reversal possible at any gate; progression criteria not pre-determined
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'phased gates' as unverified: 'Number, timing, and success criteria for each gate undefined. Who approves progression?' 'Reversible' is unverified: 'Triggers and procedures for reversal undefined.' Without defined phases and approval criteria, the trial build-up is effectively unmanaged. Progression could happen ad-hoc or be delayed indefinitely. Reversal could be triggered by minor issues or avoided despite major findings.
recommendation: Define phased gates: 'Phased trial build-up shall include [3 | 4 | 5] gates: Phase 1 [single aircraft, VLOS/EVLOS range]; Phase 2 [single aircraft, BVLOS]; Phase 3 [2-3 aircraft, coordinated]; Phase 4 [full swarm N aircraft, full autonomy]. Each phase shall progress upon [CAA approval based on: incident-free X flight-hours, evidence of [competency test | systems validation | scenario testing]]. Phase approval decision made by [CAA Programme Manager with [subject-matter expert review | independent review board]]. Reversal triggers: [incident with > severity Y | safety finding with | crew competency failure]. Reversal process: [grounded until root cause resolved | repeat phase gate].'

finding_title: Potential contradiction: 'meaningful human control throughout' vs. 'autonomous swarm decision-making'
text_excerpt: REQ010 requires 'meaningful human control...demonstrated throughout'; REQ008 requires autonomy reviewer to 'assure what the swarm will do collectively' (implying autonomous decision-making). Both cannot be fully satisfied if crew control is meaningful and continuous.
interpretation_a: Crew maintain continuous override but delegation of decision-making to autonomy is permitted within pre-defined bounds
interpretation_b: Crew have override capability but delegate most decisions to autonomy; control is meaningful but exercised selectively
reasoning: REQ010 uses 'throughout', implying continuous control. REQ008 and REQ005 describe autonomous swarm decision-making and automatic containment, implying decentralized autonomous control. These positions are in tension: full human control at all times is incompatible with autonomous swarm operations; autonomous swarms limit human meaningful control. The requirements do not resolve this contradiction.
recommendation: Clarify the autonomy model: 'The swarm shall operate under [shared control model]: (1) Crew authorize mission phases and set high-level goals; (2) Autonomy makes [tactical de-confliction | emergent response] decisions within [airborne limits]; (3) Crew retain override for [phase change | safety-critical decisions | manual intervention ≤ X seconds]. Define 'meaningful control' as [crew can perceive state via telemetry ≤ X Hz], [understand autonomy decision via telemetry ≤ Y Hz], [intervene within ≤ Z seconds]. Autonomy decisions not requiring crew approval: [coordinated avoidance, re-routing, speed adjustment]. Decisions requiring crew approval: [mission abort, geofence change].'

finding_title: Unclear relationship between 'automatic containment' and 'independent termination'
text_excerpt: REQ005 requires 'automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS'; REQ011 requires 'independent termination...so that the trial can be made safe in any off-nominal state'. Both are safety-critical; their relationship and scope division is undefined.
interpretation_a: Containment prevents exit (hard boundary enforcement); termination ensures safe landing if containment fails
interpretation_b: Both containment and termination are redundant measures; relationship undefined (are they independent? overlapping? hierarchical?)
reasoning: Both requirements address aircraft safety in the event of loss of control or out-of-bounds flight, but they seem to operate at different layers. Containment is preventive (keep aircraft in bounds); termination is corrective (land safely if in-bounds control fails). However, the requirements do not clarify this hierarchy or their interaction.
recommendation: Clarify the relationship: 'Containment and termination are two independent layers of flight safety: (1) Containment (REQ005) is the primary layer, enforcing [hard geofence] to prevent inadvertent exit. (2) Termination (REQ011) is the secondary layer, triggered if [containment fails | aircraft exits bounds | loss-of-signal timer expires | autonomy watchdog fails]. Each layer shall function independently. Verification: test both layers in isolation and in combination per [DO-178C].'

finding_title: Structural completeness gap: user stories absent for REQ017-020
text_excerpt: REQ017 (Public/Stakeholder Engagement): 'No user story covers community consultation press/media handling or prior-notification to landowners. Strongly implied by CAP 1616 and GDPR but absent.' REQ018-020: No user stories for Security Classification Handling, Environmental Impact, Post-Trial Assurance & Close-out.
interpretation_a: These requirements are documented but user stories are missing; responsibility and success criteria undefined
interpretation_b: These requirements are informally implied by external standards (CAP 1616, GDPR, CAP 722H) without explicit acceptance criteria
reasoning: REQ017-020 have titles but no user stories, success criteria, or accountable roles. Stakeholder engagement, security, environmental risk, and close-out are critical to regulatory approval but are not expressed as testable requirements. Without user stories, there is no actor (who is responsible?), no goal (what must be achieved?), and no verification (how will we know when it's done?).
recommendation: Create explicit user stories for each: REQ017: 'As a CAA authorisation officer, I want a documented stakeholder engagement plan covering [community notification, press/media response, landowner coordination] so that I can approve the trial in compliance with CAP 1616 and GDPR.' REQ018: 'As a CAA/MOD liaison, I want a classified evidence handling procedure per CAP 722H and JSP 440 so that MOD and CAA can review safety cases without security compromise.' REQ019: 'As an Environmental Assessment Officer, I want evidence of environmental impact assessment [noise, wildlife, battery risk] so that I can approve trial location and flight patterns.' REQ020: 'As a CAA Programme Manager, I want post-trial lessons-learned documentation and evidence preservation plan so that we can close out the authorization and inform future trials.'

finding_title: Undefined reference: 'the swarm'—composition and coordination not specified
text_excerpt: Requirements frequently refer to 'the swarm', 'swarm de-confliction', 'swarm decision-making' without defining swarm composition, size, coordination mechanism, or distinction from 'formation' or 'multi-agent system'.
interpretation_a: Swarm = 3-4 coordinated aircraft with wireless inter-vehicle comms; real-time cooperative de-confliction
interpretation_b: Swarm = N aircraft (unbounded) with pre-planned flight paths; minimal runtime coordination
reasoning: Dorothy glossary marks 'swarm' as unverified: 'Core concept but boundaries undefined. Minimum/maximum aircraft count? Coordination mechanism? Distinct from formation or multi-agent system?' Different interpretations imply different autonomy, communication, and failure-mode requirements. A 4-aircraft swarm with wireless comms has different assurance challenges than a 20-aircraft swarm with pre-planned routes.
recommendation: Define swarm: 'For this trial, swarm shall mean [N = 2 to 5 | N = defined per phase gate] aircraft operating under [centralized | distributed] control with coordination via [wireless inter-vehicle link | pre-planned routes | hybrid model]. Swarm shall coordinate on: [collision avoidance, formation keeping, task allocation]. Swarm shall be distinct from formation flying (which is fixed geometry) and multi-agent system (which may lack real-time coordination).'
